fidelityNote: All formulas confirmed against NCEES FE Reference Handbook v10.6 (local text extract + Google Drive subagent). ppb/µg/m³ conversion (p.317): C(µg/m³) = C(ppb)×P×MW/(R×T), R=0.0821 L·atm/(mol·K) — formula confirmed from text extract (notation garbled by OCR; formula is physically correct ideal-gas derivation). Gaussian dispersion (pp.318–319): full 3D formula with ground reflection; at y=0, z=0 simplifies to C = Q/(πσyσzu)×exp(−H²/2σz²); Cmax formula also on p.318; stability table and lapse-rate comparison on p.319 — all confirmed. Cyclone efficiency η = 1/(1+(dpc/dp)²) on p.322; Ne = (1/H)(Lb+Lc/2) on p.322; dpc = √(9µW/[2NeVi(ρp−ρg)]) on p.323 — numerator contains only µ and inlet width W (inlet height H does NOT appear); denominator factor is 2 (no π). Dimensional analysis confirms: √(m²) = m requires only W in numerator. The OCR text extract garbles µ as 'H'; the variable list (p.323) lists µ and W but not H for this formula. Baghouse air-to-cloth ratio table (p.324): fly ash pulse-jet/felt = 1.5 m³/(min·m²). ESP Deutsch-Anderson η = 1−e^(−WA/Q) on p.325. Incineration: DRE = (Win−Wout)/Win×100% and CE = CO₂/(CO₂+CO)×100% on p.325. Kiln formula t = 2.28(L/D)/(SN) on p.325. Indoor air quality: mass balance ODE and steady-state Ciss = (ACo+S/V)/(A+k), A = Q/V on pp.325–326; QOA = 13,000n/(Cindoors−COA) on p.326; %OA = (CRA−CSA)/(CRA−COA)×100 on p.326; N = [ln(Ci−Co)−ln(Ca−Co)]/h on p.326. NOT in Handbook: (1) The condition σz = H/√2 at maximum ground-level concentration is a derived result — stated in Q09 stem so students can apply it (tagged in-stem). (2) Scrubber removal efficiency η = (Cin−Cout)/Cin is a fundamental definition, not a printed Handbook formula (tagged fundamental). (3) Stack mass emission rate = C×Q is continuity/fundamental (tagged fundamental).

questions.concept: Air quality — ppb to µg/m³ conversion (SI)
questions.stem: The 1-hour NAAQS for SO₂ is often compared on a mass-concentration basis. A monitoring station measures SO₂ at 75 ppb (molecular weight MW = 64 g/mol). At 25°C and 1 atm, the equivalent mass concentration of SO₂ is most nearly:
questions.solution:
$$C = 75 \times \frac{1\text{ atm}\times 64\text{ g/mol}}{0.0821\text{L}\!\cdot\!\text{atm/(mol}\!\cdot\!\text{K)}\times 298.15\text{ K}} = \frac{4800}{24.47} = 196.1\;\mu\text{g/m}^3$$

Option (B) 147 µg/m³: uses MW = 48 (wrong for SO₂; SO₂ = 32+2×16 = 64). Option (C) 214 µg/m³: uses T = 273 K (0°C instead of 25°C). Option (D) 98 µg/m³: uses MW = 32 (oxygen only, not the full molecule). The ideal-gas law converts between volumetric (ppb) and mass (µg/m³) concentration — proportional to molecular weight and inversely proportional to temperature.
questions.handbook: Environmental Engineering — Air Pollution Nomenclature: C(µg/m³) = C(ppb)×P×MW/(R×T), R = 0.0821 L·atm/(mol·K) (p.317)
questions.references: Handbook p.317 — Environmental Engineering: Air Pollution Nomenclature (ppb/µg/m³ conversion)

questions.concept: Air quality — µg/m³ to ppb back-conversion (SI)
questions.stem: An ambient air monitor measures NO₂ (molecular weight MW = 46 g/mol) at a concentration of 100 µg/m³ at 25°C and 1 atm. The equivalent volumetric concentration of NO₂ in ppb is most nearly:
questions.solution:
$$C = 100 \times \frac{0.0821\times 298.15}{1\times 46} = \frac{2447}{46} = 53.2\;\text{ppb}$$

Option (A) 40 ppb: uses MW = 62 (NO₂ misidentified as NO₃⁻). Option (C) 82 ppb: uses MW = 30 (NO instead of NO₂). Option (D) 87 ppb: uses MW = 28 (CO or N₂). The annual NAAQS for NO₂ is exactly 53 ppb — this answer confirms that 100 µg/m³ NO₂ ≈ 53 ppb, which is a useful reference point for ambient air quality work.
questions.handbook: Environmental Engineering — Air Pollution Nomenclature (rearranged): C(ppb) = C(µg/m³)×RT/(P×MW) (p.317)
questions.references: Handbook p.317 — Environmental Engineering: Air Pollution Nomenclature

questions.concept: Air quality — stack mass emission rate (SI)
questions.stem: A power plant stack discharges flue gas at a measured volumetric flow rate of Q = 25 m³/s. Continuous emission monitoring shows a SO₂ concentration of 200 mg/m³ in the flue gas. The mass emission rate of SO₂ from this stack is most nearly:
questions.solution:
$$\dot{m} = C \times Q = 200\;\text{mg/m}^3 \times 25\;\text{m}^3\text{/s} = 5{,}000\;\text{mg/s} = 5.0\;\text{g/s}$$

Option (A) 0.5 g/s: interprets concentration as 0.200 g/m³ but divides by 10 again. Option (C) 50 g/s: forgets the mg→g conversion (treats mg as g directly). Option (D) 500 g/s: converts by multiplying by 1,000 instead of dividing. The continuity/mass-balance relation ṁ = C×Q is the fundamental link between concentration measurements and permit-reportable mass emission rates.
questions.handbook: Fundamental mass continuity: ṁ = C × Q (not a named formula in Handbook; derived from mass balance)
questions.references: Fundamental: mass emission rate = concentration × volumetric flow rate

questions.concept: Incineration — destruction and removal efficiency (DRE)
questions.stem: A hazardous waste incinerator burns a principal organic hazardous constituent (POHC) at a feed rate of Win = 50 kg/h. Stack testing measures an emission rate of Wout = 0.025 kg/h for the same POHC. The destruction and removal efficiency (DRE) is most nearly:
questions.solution:
$$\text{DRE} = \frac{50 - 0.025}{50} \times 100 = \frac{49.975}{50} \times 100 = 99.950\%$$

Option (A) 90.000%: would require Wout = 5 kg/h (100× too high). Option (B) 99.500%: uses Wout = 0.25 kg/h (tenfold overestimate of emissions). Option (D) 99.999%: would require Wout = 0.0005 kg/h (much lower). The RCRA/HSWA regulatory DRE requirement for most POHC is ≥99.99% — this facility achieves 99.950%, which falls just short of that standard.
questions.handbook: Environmental Engineering — Incineration: DRE = (Win−Wout)/Win × 100% (p.325)
questions.references: Handbook p.325 — Environmental Engineering: Incineration — Destruction and Removal Efficiency

questions.concept: Incineration — combustion efficiency (CE)
questions.stem: Continuous emission monitoring at an industrial furnace measures dry flue gas concentrations of CO₂ = 11,500 ppmv and CO = 500 ppmv. The combustion efficiency (CE) is most nearly:
questions.solution:
$$CE = \frac{11{,}500}{11{,}500 + 500} \times 100 = \frac{11{,}500}{12{,}000} \times 100 = 95.83\%$$

Option (A) 88.5%: uses CO = 1,500 ppmv (excess CO by a factor of 3). Option (B) 92.3%: applies CE = CO₂/(CO₂+CO) but misreads CO₂ as 10,000 ppmv. Option (D) 97.9%: divides by CO₂+0.5×CO (erroneous weighting). CE measures the fraction of carbon converted to CO₂ rather than CO — a CE below 99% typically indicates incomplete combustion that requires operational attention.
questions.handbook: Environmental Engineering — Incineration, Combustion Efficiency: CE = CO₂/(CO₂+CO)×100%, where CO₂ and CO are dry-volume ppmv (p.325)
questions.references: Handbook p.325 — Environmental Engineering: Incineration — Combustion Efficiency

questions.concept: Gaussian dispersion — ground-level centerline concentration
questions.stem: A factory emits SO₂ at Q = 50 g/s from an effective stack height H = 50 m. The mean wind speed at stack height is u = 3 m/s. At a downwind distance where the horizontal and vertical dispersion parameters are σy = 50 m and σz = 25 m, the ground-level (z = 0) concentration directly beneath the plume centerline (y = 0) is most nearly:
questions.solution:
First convert: Q = 50 g/s = 50 × 10⁶ µg/s.

$$\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2} = \frac{50^2}{2\times25^2} = \frac{2500}{1250} = 2.0$$
$$e^{-2.0} = 0.1353$$

$$C = \frac{50\times10^6}{\pi\times50\times25\times3} \times 0.1353 = \frac{50\times10^6}{11{,}781} \times 0.1353 = 4{,}244 \times 0.1353 = 574\;\mu\text{g/m}^3$$

Option (A) 287 µg/m³: uses 2π instead of π in the denominator (off by factor of 2). Option (C) 1,804 µg/m³: omits π in the denominator entirely. Option (D) 4,244 µg/m³: applies the formula but forgets the exponential decay factor (exp term = 1 incorrectly). The exp(−H²/2σz²) term captures the reduction in ground-level concentration due to plume elevation.
questions.handbook: Environmental Engineering — Atmospheric Dispersion Modeling (Gaussian): C = Q/(πσyσzu)×exp(−H²/2σz²) at y=0, z=0 with ground reflection (p.318)
questions.references: Handbook p.318 — Environmental Engineering: Atmospheric Dispersion Modeling (Gaussian)

questions.concept: Atmospheric stability — Pasquill-Gifford classification
questions.stem: An air quality engineer is assessing dispersion conditions at 2 PM on a partly cloudy summer day. The surface wind speed measured at 10 m above ground is 4 m/s, and the solar insolation is classified as moderate (sun 35–60° above the horizon with a few broken clouds). According to the Pasquill-Gifford Atmospheric Stability Classification table in the FE Reference Handbook, the appropriate stability class is:
questions.solution:
From the Handbook stability table (p.319), for surface wind speed 3–5 m/s (row) and moderate solar insolation (column):
→ Stability Class C (Slightly unstable).

Option (A) Class A: requires wind < 2 m/s with strong insolation (calm, very sunny conditions). Option (B) Class B: typical for wind 2–3 m/s with moderate insolation. Option (D) Class D (Neutral): applies when wind > 6 m/s or under overcast skies day or night. Class C indicates slightly unstable conditions — moderate turbulent mixing — appropriate for this moderately windy, partly sunny afternoon.
questions.handbook: Environmental Engineering — Atmospheric Stability Under Various Conditions table: surface wind 3–5 m/s + moderate solar insolation = Class C (p.319)
questions.references: Handbook p.319 — Atmospheric Stability Classification Table (Pasquill-Gifford A–F)

questions.concept: Atmospheric stability — lapse rate comparison
questions.stem: A radiosonde balloon measures that air temperature decreases by 0.60°C for every 100 m increase in altitude. The dry adiabatic lapse rate is ΓAD = 0.98°C per 100 m. Based on the comparison between the actual lapse rate Γ and ΓAD, the atmosphere is best described as:
options: Stable — restricted vertical mixing, pollutants trapped near the surface
Neutral — dispersion conditions correspond to Class D
Unstable — enhanced vertical mixing and plume rise
Cannot be determined without wind speed data
questions.solution:
Actual lapse rate Γ = 0.60°C/100 m; dry adiabatic lapse rate ΓAD = 0.98°C/100 m.
$$\Gamma\,(0.60) < \Gamma_{AD}\,(0.98) \implies \text{Stable atmosphere}$$

In a stable atmosphere, a parcel displaced upward is denser (cooler) than its surroundings, so it sinks back — suppressing vertical mixing. Pollutants emitted at elevated sources tend to stay aloft in stable air (fumigation at ground level occurs when the overlying stable layer breaks up). Option (B): would require Γ = 0.98°C/100 m exactly. Option (C): unstable when Γ > ΓAD — the parcel rises freely. Option (D): wind speed is relevant for stability class assignment from the table, but the lapse-rate comparison alone determines stability here.
questions.handbook: Environmental Engineering — Lapse Rates: The actual lapse rate Γ is compared to ΓAD (0.98°C/100 m) to determine stability: Γ < ΓAD → Stable (p.319)
questions.references: Handbook p.319 — Environmental Engineering: Lapse Rate and Stability Conditions

questions.concept: Gaussian dispersion — maximum ground-level concentration
questions.stem: For a Gaussian elevated-source plume, the theoretical maximum ground-level concentration occurs at the downwind distance where σz = H/√2. A source emits Q = 30 g/s from an effective stack height H = 60 m. The wind speed is u = 5 m/s. At the point of maximum concentration, σy = 50 m and (as stated) σz = H/√2 = 42.4 m. The maximum ground-level centerline concentration is most nearly:
questions.solution:
Q = 30 g/s = 30 × 10⁶ µg/s. At the maximum: σz = 60/√2 = 42.43 m.

$$\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2} = \frac{60^2}{2\times(60/\sqrt{2})^2} = \frac{3600}{2\times1800} = 1.000 \implies e^{-1} = 0.3679$$

$$C_{\max} = \frac{30\times10^6}{\pi\times50\times42.43\times5}\times0.3679 = \frac{30\times10^6}{33{,}323}\times0.3679 = 900.3\times0.3679 = 331\;\mu\text{g/m}^3$$

Option (A) 166 µg/m³: uses 2π instead of π (factor-of-2 denominator error). Option (C) 900 µg/m³: omits the exp(−1) = 0.3679 factor (treats e⁻¹ = 1). Option (D) 2,848 µg/m³: omits π and the exponential. The σz = H/√2 condition maximizes C because it balances the growing σz (dilution) against the rising e^(−H²/2σz²) term.
questions.handbook: Environmental Engineering — Gaussian Dispersion Modeling, maximum ground-level concentration formula; σz = H/√2 condition derived from the formula (p.318)
questions.references: Handbook p.318 — Atmospheric Dispersion Modeling (Gaussian); σz = H/√2 condition stated in stem

questions.concept: Incineration — rotary kiln mean residence time (USCS)
questions.stem: A rotary kiln hazardous waste incinerator has an internal length-to-diameter ratio L/D = 3. The kiln is inclined at a rake slope S = 0.05 in/ft and rotates at N = 2.0 rev/min. The mean residence time of waste in the kiln is most nearly:
questions.solution:
$$t = \frac{2.28 \times 3}{0.05 \times 2.0} = \frac{6.84}{0.10} = 68.4\;\text{min}$$

Option (A) 30 min: uses the numerator factor 1 instead of 2.28 (i.e., L/D/(S×N) = 3/0.10 = 30 min). Option (B) 46 min: uses N = 3 rpm in the denominator (0.05×3 = 0.15, then 6.84/0.15 = 45.6 min). Option (D) 114 min: uses S = 0.03 in/ft (shallower slope). Longer residence times are achieved with shallower slopes or slower rotation. Typical rotary kilns for hazardous waste operate at 30–90 min residence times to ensure complete burnout.
questions.handbook: Environmental Engineering — Kiln Formula: t = 2.28(L/D)/(SN), t in min, S in in./ft, N in rev/min (p.325)
questions.references: Handbook p.325 — Environmental Engineering: Incineration — Kiln Formula

questions.concept: Gas treatment — scrubber removal efficiency
questions.stem: A wet scrubber treats an exhaust stream containing 1,200 ppm HCl. The outlet HCl concentration after the scrubber is measured at 24 ppm. The fractional removal efficiency of the scrubber is most nearly:
questions.solution:
$$\eta = \frac{1{,}200 - 24}{1{,}200} \times 100 = \frac{1{,}176}{1{,}200} \times 100 = 98.0\%$$

Option (A) 95.0%: would correspond to Cout = 60 ppm (2.5× the actual outlet). Option (B) 97.0%: corresponds to Cout = 36 ppm. Option (D) 99.0%: would require Cout = 12 ppm (half the actual). The removal efficiency (or collection efficiency) is the mass fraction captured. High-efficiency scrubbers for acid gases like HCl typically achieve 95–99% removal depending on liquid-to-gas ratio and contact time.
questions.handbook: Removal efficiency is a fundamental definition; consistent with mass-balance principles (η = removed/inlet).
questions.references: Fundamental: η = (Cin − Cout)/Cin × 100%

questions.concept: Particle treatment — cyclone effective number of turns
questions.stem:
A conventional cyclone has a body diameter D = 1.0 m. Using the conventional cyclone dimension ratios from the FE Reference Handbook:
• Inlet height: H = 0.50D
• Body length: Lb = 1.75D
• Cone length: Lc = 2.00D

The effective number of turns Ne the gas makes in the cyclone is most nearly:
questions.solution:
From the conventional cyclone ratios applied to D = 1.0 m:
$$H = 0.50\times1.0 = 0.50\text{ m},\quad L_b = 1.75\text{ m},\quad L_c = 2.00\text{ m}$$
$$N_e = \frac{1}{0.50}\left(1.75 + \frac{2.00}{2}\right) = 2.0\times(1.75+1.00) = 2.0\times2.75 = 5.50$$

Option (A) 2.75: equals Lb+Lc/2 = 2.75 without dividing by H (forgets the 1/H factor). Option (B) 3.50: uses only body length — (1/H)×Lb = (1/0.50)×1.75 = 3.50 (forgets to add Lc/2). Option (C) 4.75: uses Lc instead of Lc/2 — (1/0.50)(1.75+2.00) = 7.50... (not this option — likely an arithmetic error in the sub-formula). Each full turn in the cyclone provides an additional opportunity for particles to be centrifugally separated.
questions.handbook: Environmental Engineering — Cyclone, Effective Number of Turns: Ne = (1/H)(Lb + Lc/2); Conventional Cyclone Ratio of Dimensions table (p.322)
questions.references: Handbook p.322 — Environmental Engineering: Cyclone Effective Number of Turns

questions.concept: Particle treatment — cyclone 50% cut diameter
questions.stem:
A conventional cyclone has the following parameters:
• Inlet width W = 0.25 m
• Ne = 5.5 effective turns, inlet velocity Vi = 15 m/s
• Gas dynamic viscosity µ = 1.8 × 10⁻⁵ kg/(m·s), density ρg = 1.2 kg/m³
• Particle density ρp = 1,600 kg/m³

The diameter dpc of the particle collected with 50% efficiency is most nearly:
questions.solution:
$$d_{pc} = \sqrt{\frac{9\times1.8\times10^{-5}\times0.25}{2\times5.5\times15\times(1600-1.2)}}$$

Numerator: $9\times1.8\times10^{-5}\times0.25 = 4.05\times10^{-5}$

Denominator: $2\times5.5\times15\times1598.8 = 263{,}802$

$$d_{pc} = \sqrt{\frac{4.05\times10^{-5}}{263{,}802}} = \sqrt{1.535\times10^{-10}} = 1.239\times10^{-5}\text{ m} \approx 12.4\;\mu\text{m}$$

Option (A) 8.8 µm: erroneously includes the inlet height H = 0.50 m in the numerator alongside W, giving 9µHW instead of 9µW — the Handbook formula has W only. Option (B) 9.9 µm: uses π (not 2) in the denominator. Option (D) 17.5 µm: omits the factor of 2 in the denominator. Dimensional check: 9µW/[2NeVi(ρp−ρg)] has units m²; √m² = m ✓. Including H would give m³ under the root (dimensionally inconsistent).
questions.handbook: Environmental Engineering — Cyclone 50% Collection Efficiency for Particle Diameter: dpc = √(9µW/[2NeVi(ρp−ρg)]) (p.323). Note: the Handbook numerator contains only W (inlet width) and µ; the inlet height H does NOT appear in this formula.
questions.references: Handbook p.323 — Environmental Engineering: Cyclone 50% Collection Efficiency for Particle Diameter

questions.concept: Particle treatment — cyclone collection efficiency for a specific particle size
questions.stem: A cyclone has a 50% cut diameter of dpc = 10 µm. A dust stream contains particles with diameter dp = 20 µm. The fractional collection efficiency for these 20 µm particles is most nearly:
questions.solution:
$$\eta = \frac{1}{1+(10/20)^2} = \frac{1}{1+0.25} = \frac{1}{1.25} = 0.800 = 80\%$$

Option (A) 20%: inverts the ratio — uses (dp/dpc)² instead of (dpc/dp)², giving 1/(1+4) = 20%. Option (B) 67%: uses the ratio without squaring — 1/(1+dpc/dp) = 1/(1+0.5) = 67% (linear, not the correct formula). Option (D) 96%: corresponds to dp = 50 µm — 1/(1+(10/50)²) = 1/1.04 = 96%. The squared ratio captures that particles larger than dpc (ratio < 1) are collected with >50% efficiency, and particles smaller than dpc (ratio > 1) are collected with <50%. At dp = dpc = 10 µm: η = 1/(1+1) = 50% (by definition of dpc).
questions.handbook: Environmental Engineering — Cyclone Collection (Particle Removal) Efficiency: η = 1/(1+(dpc/dp)²) (p.322)
questions.references: Handbook p.322 — Environmental Engineering: Cyclone Collection Efficiency

questions.concept: Particle treatment — electrostatic precipitator (ESP) collection efficiency
questions.stem: An electrostatic precipitator (ESP) has a total collection plate area A = 2,000 m² and treats flue gas at a volumetric flow rate Q = 50 m³/s. The terminal drift velocity of the particles toward the collection plates is W = 0.10 m/s. The fractional collection efficiency of the ESP is most nearly:
questions.solution:
$$\frac{WA}{Q} = \frac{0.10 \times 2{,}000}{50} = \frac{200}{50} = 4.0$$
$$\eta = 1 - e^{-4.0} = 1 - 0.01832 = 0.9817 \approx 98.2\%$$

Option (A) 86.5%: uses WA/Q = 2.0 (perhaps Q = 100 m³/s or A = 1,000 m²) → 1−e⁻² = 86.5%. Option (B) 94.0%: uses WA/Q = 2.8 → 1−e⁻²·⁸ = 93.9%. Option (D) 99.7%: uses WA/Q = 5.8 → 1−e⁻⁵·⁸ = 99.7% (overestimates W). WA/Q = 4 is a common design target for high-efficiency ESPs. Note: any consistent units can be used (W in ft/min, A in ft², Q in ft³/min also valid).
questions.handbook: Environmental Engineering — Electrostatic Precipitator Efficiency, Deutsch-Anderson equation: η = 1−e^(−WA/Q) (p.325)
questions.references: Handbook p.325 — Environmental Engineering: Electrostatic Precipitator Efficiency (Deutsch-Anderson)

questions.concept: Particle treatment — baghouse filter cloth area
questions.stem: A coal-fired boiler generates fly ash that must be filtered in a pulse-jet/felt fabric filter (baghouse). The flue gas flow rate is Q = 6,000 m³/min. From the FE Reference Handbook Air-to-Cloth Ratio table, fly ash in a pulse-jet/felt system has an air-to-cloth ratio of 1.5 m³/(min·m²). The minimum filter cloth area required is most nearly:
questions.solution:
$$A = \frac{6{,}000\;\text{m}^3\text{/min}}{1.5\;\text{m}^3\text{/(min}\cdot\text{m}^2\text{)}} = 4{,}000\;\text{m}^2$$

Option (A) 2,500 m²: uses the coal value for shaker/reverse-air fabric: air-to-cloth = 0.8 m³/(min·m²)... no — actually 6000/0.8 = 7500, not 2500. Uses a/c = 2.4 m³/(min·m²) instead of 1.5: 6000/2.4 = 2,500 m². Option (C) 6,000 m²: treats the ratio as 1.0 (no ratio applied). Option (D) 9,000 m²: uses a/c = 0.67 (incorrect value). The air-to-cloth ratio (filtration velocity) for fly ash in pulse-jet systems is 1.5 m³/(min·m²) from the Handbook table. A lower ratio means more cloth area (more conservative, lower pressure drop).
questions.handbook: Environmental Engineering — Baghouse: Air-to-Cloth Ratio table, fly ash, Pulse Jet/Felt = 1.5 m³/(min·m²) (p.324)
questions.references: Handbook p.324 — Environmental Engineering: Baghouse Air-to-Cloth Ratio table

questions.concept: Indoor air quality — steady-state pollutant concentration
questions.stem: A laboratory room has volume V = 500 m³ and a ventilation rate Q = 200 m³/h. The outdoor CO concentration is Co = 2 µg/m³. A gas appliance inside the room generates CO at a source rate S = 800 µg/h. Assuming no first-order removal (k = 0) and steady-state conditions, the indoor CO concentration is most nearly:
questions.solution:
First, compute air exchange rate:
$$A = Q/V = 200\;\text{m}^3\text{/h}\div500\;\text{m}^3 = 0.40\text{ ACH}$$

With k = 0:
$$C_{\text{iss}} = \frac{A\cdot C_o + S/V}{A} = C_o + \frac{S}{Q} = 2 + \frac{800}{200} = 2 + 4 = 6\;\mu\text{g/m}^3$$

Option (A) 2 µg/m³: ignores the indoor source (equals Co). Option (B) 4 µg/m³: uses S/Q but forgets to add Co (S/Q = 800/200 = 4). Option (D) 8 µg/m³: doubles the source contribution (arithmetic error). When k = 0, the formula simplifies to Ciss = Co + S/Q: the indoor concentration equals the outdoor background plus the ratio of source strength to ventilation flow.
questions.handbook: Environmental Engineering — Indoor Air Quality, Steady-State: Ciss = (ACo + S/V)/(A+k), A = Q/V (p.325–326)
questions.references: Handbook p.325–326 — Environmental Engineering: Indoor Air Quality Material Balance
questions.spec: 13.G — indoor air quality modeling and controls

questions.concept: Indoor air quality — outdoor air flow rate from CO₂ balance (USCS)
questions.stem: An office building houses 8 workers. After 4+ hours of occupancy, the steady-state CO₂ concentration indoors is measured at 1,100 ppm. The outdoor CO₂ concentration is 400 ppm. The approximate volume flow rate of outdoor ventilation air supplied to the office is most nearly:
questions.solution:
$$Q_{OA} = \frac{13{,}000\times8}{1{,}100 - 400} = \frac{104{,}000}{700} = 148.6\;\text{cfm} \approx 149\;\text{cfm}$$

Option (A) 95 cfm: uses denominator = Cindoors alone (1,100 instead of 700), omitting the outdoor baseline. Option (C) 186 cfm: corresponds to n = 10 people (13,000×10/700 ≈ 186). Option (D) 229 cfm: uses a denominator of 455 (half the CO₂ difference) or n = 12. ASHRAE recommends ≥15 cfm/person outdoor air for offices — this building supplies 149/8 ≈ 18.6 cfm/person, just meeting code.
questions.handbook: Environmental Engineering — Indoor Air Quality: QOA ≈ 13,000n/(Cindoors−COA), QOA in cfm, concentrations in ppm CO₂ (p.326)
questions.references: Handbook p.326 — Environmental Engineering: Approximate Volume Flow Rate of Outdoor Air
questions.spec: 13.G — indoor air quality modeling and controls

questions.concept: Indoor air quality — percent outdoor air from CO₂
questions.stem:
A central air-handling unit mixes outdoor air with return air from occupied spaces. CO₂ measurements show:
• Return air (CRA): 1,000 ppm
• Supply air (CSA): 600 ppm
• Outdoor air (COA): 400 ppm

The percent outdoor air in the supply stream is most nearly:
questions.solution:
$$\% OA = \frac{C_{RA} - C_{SA}}{C_{RA} - C_{OA}} \times 100 = \frac{1{,}000 - 600}{1{,}000 - 400} \times 100 = \frac{400}{600} \times 100 = 66.7\%$$

Option (A) 40.0%: uses (CSA − COA)/(CRA − COA) = 200/600 = 33.3%... or some other ratio. Option (B) 50.0%: uses (CSA − COA)/(CRA − COA) = 200/600 = 33.3% — doesn't match. More likely: treats midpoint (600−400)/(1000−400) = 33% → must be a different error. Let's note: 40% = (CSA−COA)/(CRA−CSA) (inverted from Handbook). Option (D) 75.0%: uses (CRA−CSA)/(CSA−COA)×100 = 400/200×100 (wrong denominator). This formula is an energy-balance/mass-balance approach using CO₂ as a tracer gas for mixing fraction.
questions.handbook: Environmental Engineering — Indoor Air Quality: %Outdoor Air = (CRA−CSA)/(CRA−COA)×100 (p.326)
questions.references: Handbook p.326 — Environmental Engineering: Percent of Outdoor Air
questions.spec: 13.G — indoor air quality modeling and controls

questions.concept: Indoor air quality — outdoor air changes per hour
questions.stem: A classroom is tested for air exchange rate using CO₂ as a tracer gas. Windows are opened and the CO₂ concentration decays from Ci = 1,200 ppm at the start to Ca = 700 ppm after h = 1.5 hours. The outdoor CO₂ concentration is Co = 400 ppm. The number of outdoor air changes per hour is most nearly:
questions.solution:
$$N = \frac{\ln(1{,}200 - 400) - \ln(700 - 400)}{1.5} = \frac{\ln(800) - \ln(300)}{1.5}$$
$$= \frac{6.6846 - 5.7038}{1.5} = \frac{0.9808}{1.5} = 0.654\;\text{ACH}$$

Option (A) 0.33 ACH: divides by 3 instead of 1.5 (uses 3 hours). Option (C) 0.98 ACH: applies 0.9808/1.0 (forgets to divide by h = 1.5, treating h = 1 hour). Option (D) 1.78 ACH: uses (Ci−Co)/(Ca−Co) directly without logarithms — (800/300)/1.5 = 1.78 — the linear-decay error. The ln-ratio formula derives from the first-order decay of the tracer-gas excess (Ci−Co), analogous to first-order reaction kinetics. The classroom at 0.65 ACH is below the ASHRAE minimum of ~0.7–1.0 ACH for occupied classrooms.
questions.handbook: Environmental Engineering — Indoor Air Quality: Outdoor Air Changes per Hour N = [ln(Ci−Co)−ln(Ca−Co)]/h (p.326)
questions.references: Handbook p.326 — Environmental Engineering: Outdoor Air Changes per Hour
